retrieved_docs.title: A Wave -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Jugular vein pulsation (JVP) provides clinically meaningful information about the central venous pressure (CVP). JVP is an important and reproducible tool to diagnose various cardiac conditions which can cause a change in CVP. Canon A wave can be easily missed without close attention during bedside examination especially due to declining physical exam skills in contemporary clinical practice. Recent literature does not describe the frequency of cannon A waves in rhythm disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: A Wave -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A proper heart function requires a synchronized action of the myocardium. When the cardiac rhythm is disturbed, the heart cannot provide proper blood flow. Cannon A waves are an example of this. Electrical disturbances cause mechanical problems causing atrial contraction to occur when the tricuspid valve is closed. The reflection of the pressure wave travels up the venous system and can be examined in the jugular vein as exaggerated A wave pulsation. A Wave in JVP is an expression of normal atrial contraction. In opposition to Canon A waves, missing A waves might indicate atrial standstill as seen in atrial fibrillation.

retrieved_docs.title: Jugular Venous Distention -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Waveforms of JVP signify the following:
retrieved_docs.content: a - presystolic, produced by right atrial contraction c - (isovolumic phase) bulging of the tricuspid valve into the right atrium during ventricular systole v - occurs in late systole; increased blood in the right atrium from venous return Descents x - a combination of atrial relaxation, downward movement of the tricuspid valve, and ventricular systole y - the tricuspid valve opens, and the blood flows into the right ventricle.

retrieved_docs.title: A Wave -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The venous pressure curve can be altered in different ways and Cannon A waves may be mistaken. For example, giant C and V waves can occur in tricuspid regurgitation. This is called Lancisi's sign.  Jugular vein distension can result from a pulmonary embolism as part of Beck's Triad. Elevated JVP without venous pulsations should raise the suspicion of superior vena cava obstruction. This illustrates the importance of venous pressure as a window to the right heart.

retrieved_docs.title: Pacemaker Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Hypotension, tachypnea, low oxygen saturation, lung rales Narrow pulse pressure and low pulse volume Neck vein distension and cannon wave in JVP tracing Precordial examination may reveal regurgitant murmurs and variability in the intensity of first heart sound Pulsatile liver and ascites may be present in severe cases Lower extremity edema Neurologic examination may reveal confusion, dizziness, or altered mental status

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Jugular Venous Pulsation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A critical bedside examination of the JVP provides invaluable information relating to disease processes and medication management. Using the method above to examine the height of the JVP, clinicians are able to interpret the volume status of patients. Simply stated, an elevated JVP of greater than 9 cm H 2 O (venous hypertension) along with other symptoms can help distinguish between left and right heart failure, suggest pericardial disease, and suggest some specific types of arrhythmias. [4] Conversely, a low JVP of less than 5 cm H 2 O can reflect either hypovolemia or the use of diuretics. The JVP assessment used alongside other diagnostic cardiac testing can help the clinician to make the proper diagnosis and guide treatment decisions. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Jugular Venous Distention -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pathological conditions associated with abnormal waveforms:
retrieved_docs.content: A large 'v' wave is seen in tricuspid regurgitation when the right ventricle contracts and the tricuspid valve does not completely close, which results in the shooting of blood back to the right atrium. JVP is one of the most common and readily available tools to measure right atrial pressure (RAP) and can be used to assess volume status, especially hypervolemic states, due to cardiac etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Jugular Venous Pulsation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The "Y" descent can also be significantly attenuated because of the inability of the right atrium to relax during ventricular diastole. [24] The fixed perimeter of the fluid-filled pericardial sac results in increased pressure on the right atrium during its emptying of blood into the right ventricle. Although there is blood leaving the atrium potentially relieving pressure on the atrium, this relief is immediately negated by the pressure from the upward shift of fluid within the pericardial sac as a result of the expansion of the right ventricle. [25] [26] [27] This phenomenon is known as cardiac tamponade. Cardiac tamponade can be characterized on a JVP tracing as a severely attenuated or absent "Y" descent. On the contrary, one can observe a very prominent "Y descent' (Friedreich's sign) with constrictive pericarditis due to the limited ability of the right ventricle to expand during ventricular diastole. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Jugular Venous Distention -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Waveforms of JVP signify the following:
retrieved_docs.content: When these are transmitted to the skin, a series of flickers are visible throughout the overlying skin. Once we have identified the IJV, we must estimate the venous pressure (VP). Normal VP is 1 to 8 cm of water.

retrieved_docs.title: A Wave -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: When inspecting the patient's neck, a variety of pulsation phenomena might be seen. The important differentials of elevated JVP are fluid overload due to variety of causes, superior vena cava obstruction, or an increase in intrathoracic pressure.  The first step is to differentiate between arterial and venous pulsations by location and strength on palpation. There are higher pressures existent in the arterial than in the venous circulation. Right Internal jugular vein is usually preferred over the left as it is in direct line with superior vena cava.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Ventricular Hypertrophy -- History and Physical -- Jugular vein assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Distended and prominent jugular veins are apparent and reflect right atrial pressure elevation. A distinct “c-v” wave in jugular venous pulse measurements is due to systolic regurgitation through the tricuspid valve into the right atrium. RVH also produces a prominent jugular venous pulse “a” wave, often accompanied by a right-sided S4 and either a left parasternal heave or downward subxiphoid thrust. Jugular venous distension may be more prominent with inspiration (Kussmaul sign) due to the increased venous return. The Kussmaul sign may not be evident if jugular venous distension is severe.

retrieved_docs.title: A Wave -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cannon A waves are related to rhythm disturbances causing changes in the normal cardiac blood flow causing a large pressure vein in the jugular vein. Different dysrhythmias may cause cannon A waves. Heart block may lead to cannon A waves, in particular, third-degree (complete) heart block. It may be seen with ventricular tachycardia (VT) as a result of the underlying atrioventricular (AV) dissociation. Another cause is Pacemaker syndrome without proper synchronization of atria and ventricles. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: A Wave -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cannon A waves need to be distinguished from giant A waves that occur in right heart structural changes such as tricuspid valvulopathies, right ventricular hypertrophy, and pulmonary hypertension. To the observer of the jugular, venous-pressure giant A waves and Cannon A waves may appear similar. From physical exams alone it might be difficult to differentiate between them.

retrieved_docs.title: A Wave -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cannon A waves are large-amplitude waves seen in the jugular veins during a physical exam. They are caused by simultaneous contraction of atria and ventricle leading to exaggerated right atrial pressure. Usually, Cannon A waves are irregular and intermittent. They are seen in patients with cardiac conduction defects or certain cardiac dysrhythmias. Cannon A waves can also be seen on the EKG. [1] [2] [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with large pericardial effusions, the Ewart sign may be present. This is an area of dullness with bronchial breath sounds heard just below the left scapula. The JVP tracing may reveal an absent 'y' descent due to the elevated intrapericardial pressure that prevents the filling of the ventricles.

retrieved_docs.title: Third-Degree Atrioventricular Block -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The physical exam is usually remarkable for bradycardia. JVP examination often demonstrates cannon A-waves owing to the simultaneous contraction of the atria and ventricles. Thus a very large pressure wave is felt up against the vein. Especially with heart rates below 40/min, patients might also present with features consistent with decompensated heart failure, respiratory distress, and hypoprofusion, such as diaphoresis, tachypnea, altered mental status, retraction, cool skin, and decreased capillary refill.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia target the intrinsic accelerated ventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and its subtype ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Understanding these interconnected entities—electrical conduction abnormalities, structural cardiac changes, specific arrhythmic syndromes, diagnostic measurements, and therapeutic interventions—is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Together, these concepts illustrate how electrocardiographic monitoring and cardiac electrophysiology studies are interrelated tools for diagnosing conduction abnormalities, assessing arrhythmic risk, guiding therapeutic ablation, and supporting cardiac rehabilitation, thereby informing comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2103
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular examination and evaluation is a comprehensive clinical domain that encompasses cardiovascular procedures. Within the category of cardiovascular procedures, specific child interventions include cardioversion, elective, electrical conversion of arrhythmia; external, demand pacing, and plethysmografie celotělová. These procedures represent both therapeutic and diagnostic components used in the assessment and management of cardiovascular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: مُعَجَّل) includes ventricular acceleration disorders (التسارع البطيني), and the electrophysiologic procedures of arrhythmia induction, focus ablation, and ventricular tachycardia treatment are specifically directed at managing ventricular tachycardia (التسارع البطيني). Together, these interconnected entities and procedures support the assessment, classification, and therapeutic intervention of cardiac electrical disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2254
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures constitute a comprehensive category of diagnostic and therapeutic monitoring techniques that encompass several specific modalities. Blood pressure monitoring and cardiotachometry are identified as child procedures within this category, reflecting their roles in assessing circulatory dynamics and cardiac rate respectively. Additional child procedures include monitoring of pacemaker regime or therapy, which evaluates the function and settings of implanted cardiac devices; monitoring of pulse, which provides real‑time assessment of arterial pulse characteristics; and phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing, which records acoustic heart sounds together with vascular flow patterns. Together, these interconnected procedures enable clinicians to obtain detailed cardiovascular information, guide treatment decisions, and ensure optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: In clinical assessment, results that are above the reference range are classified as increased findings, and an increased finding is interpreted as indicating tachycardia (تسرع القلب).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: recording. The entity ECG: non‑sinus rhythm is a subtype of electrocardiographic monitoring and its primary focus is on conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل). Likewise, ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia has a relationship with conduction disorders, highlighting the role of ECG devices in detecting and characterizing abnormal cardiac conduction. Cardiac electrophysiologic study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) is defined as the invasive evaluation that includes arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic‑focus ablation, and treatment of specific tachyarrhythmias. This study is applied to atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, and ventricular tachycardia, each being an instance of the electrophysiologic evaluation. The ventricular‑tachycardia evaluation specifically focuses on ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Together, these concepts illustrate how electrocardiographic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias, which are classified as a subcategory of تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ (supraventricular tachycardia), are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy). This connection suggests that individuals experiencing cardiac dysrhythmias often show structural changes in the left ventricle, underscoring the need to assess ventricular hypertrophy when diagnosing and treating supraventricular tachyarrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 938
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “has child” is linked to withdrawal and rebound effects, which can manifest as rebound tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition that reflects an overall increase in arterial pressure. Within the broader vascular category, several specific hypertensive disorders are recognized as subtypes, including أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). Although these entities share the common feature of elevated blood pressure, they differ in their underlying mechanisms, affected vascular territories, and clinical manifestations. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure and its vascular subtypes are interrelated helps clinicians accurately diagnose, monitor, and tailor treatment strategies for patients presenting with various forms of hypertension, thereby improving patient care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures is a comprehensive diagnostic category that encompasses several specialized techniques. Within this category, Cardiotachometry is used to assess heart rate and rhythm through pulse‑based measurements. Intracardiac phonocardiogram provides acoustic recordings from within the cardiac chambers to evaluate heart sounds directly. Monitoring of pulse is a fundamental component for evaluating circulatory status and detecting abnormalities in heart rhythm. Phonocardiogram with apex cardiogram records heart sounds at the cardiac apex, offering detailed information about valvular function and cardiac mechanics. Phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing combines heart‑sound analysis with simultaneous arterial or venous waveform monitoring, allowing integrated assessment of cardiac and vascular dynamics. Together, these procedures constitute the full spectrum of electrocardiographic and monitoring methods employed in cardiac evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are both classified as vascular abnormalities, reflecting increased pressure within the circulatory system. The broader vascular category also encompasses severe pressure disorders such as أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate a spectrum of vascular pressure elevations that are important for clinicians to recognize, as they can signal acute emergencies or chronic pulmonary vascular disease and guide appropriate monitoring and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2064
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures represent a comprehensive category of cardiovascular assessment techniques that include blood pressure monitoring, cardiotachometry, intracardiac phonocardiogram, monitoring of pacemaker (regime/therapy), and monitoring of pulse. Blood pressure monitoring provides continuous or intermittent measurement of arterial pressure, aiding in the detection of hypertension and hemodynamic instability. Cardiotachometry records the speed of cardiac impulses, offering insight into conduction velocity and rhythm disturbances. Intracardiac phonocardiogram captures acoustic signals from within the heart, allowing detailed evaluation of intracardiac sounds and murmurs. Monitoring of pacemaker (regime/therapy) tracks device function, battery status, and pacing parameters to ensure appropriate therapy delivery. Monitoring of pulse assesses peripheral pulse rate and rhythm, contributing to the overall evaluation of cardiac output and circulatory status. Together, these procedures form an integrated monitoring framework that supports thorough cardiac diagnostics, guides therapeutic decisions, and enhances patient safety in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني) is clinically associated with both supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and atrial flutter (رفرفة أذينية). These arrhythmic conditions frequently arise in the setting of congestive cardiomyopathy, reflecting a close pathophysiological link in which impaired myocardial function predisposes patients to rapid supraventricular rhythms and organized atrial flutter circuits. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of supraventricular tachycardia or atrial flutter may signal underlying congestive cardiomyopathy and guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at both the structural heart disease and its rhythm complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 628
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular procedures are categorized as a subset of other cardiovascular procedures, which in turn fall under the broader group of other diagnostic procedures. Within the domain of cardiovascular procedures, several specific interventions are identified. Cardiac pacing is recognized as a distinct cardiovascular procedure and is also classified under the methodological category of Procedure by method. Cardiovascular examination and evaluation represents another specialized cardiovascular procedure focused on assessment and diagnostic testing. Elective external cardioversion, described as the electrical conversion of arrhythmia, is listed as a child of cardiovascular procedures, as is demand pacing, a form of rate‑responsive pacing therapy. Additionally, whole‑body plethysmography (plethysmografie celotělová) is included among the cardiovascular procedures, reflecting its role in comprehensive cardiovascular assessment. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which general diagnostic categories encompass increasingly specific cardiovascular interventions, facilitating organized understanding and management of cardiovascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiogram rhythm strip telephonic or telemetric transmission serves as the primary focus of قياس القلب عن بعد (remote heart measurement), enabling clinicians to monitor cardiac rhythm remotely through telephone or telemetry technologies.
